title: Outliers: find them and decide what to do
intro: The register at "Mama John's" sees ordinary receipts — and suddenly an order for twenty pizzas: an office party. One such value can flip the mean, inflate the variance and ruin a model. Yet an outlier is not always "an error": sometimes it is the most valuable thing in the data. Let's learn to find them and, crucially, to decide deliberately what to do with them.

beats.text: An outlier is a value that has broken far away from the bulk (the red point on the right). Watch the two markers: the green mean has drifted toward the outlier, while the yellow median stayed with the main group. One extreme moves the mean — and barely touches the median.

How to find outliers. The 1.5·IQR rule: anything farther than 1.5 box-widths from Q1/Q3 (from the percentiles lesson). Or by z-score: |z| > 3 sigmas from the mean. Careful: both the mean and σ are themselves inflated by the outlier, so on tailed data the 1.5·IQR rule (built on robust quartiles) is more reliable than z.
You found an outlier — now what? First question: is it an error or reality?
beats.predict: A salary of "−5" and a salary of "director, 280" — are these the same kind of case?
beats.reveal: No. "−5" is an obvious input error (salaries below zero do not exist): fix it or drop it. "280" is a real value: deleting it means lying about the data. A real outlier is not "garbage" but a signal — possibly the most interesting part.

beats.text: Cycle through the action buttons and watch the mean and the median move.
beats.predict: Which action pulls the mean back to the main group hardest — capping, winsorizing or removing?
beats.reveal: All three remove the extreme's "pull", but differently: capping/winsorizing KEEP the observation (clipped to a sensible threshold), removal throws it away entirely — acceptable only for clear errors. The median barely moves in any case — it was robust to begin with.

beats.text: The golden rule: never delete outliers just because they are inconvenient. First understand their nature, choose a deliberate action, document it, and check how it affects the conclusion. Quietly dropping inconvenient points is exactly what fitting the data to a desired answer looks like.

definitions.text: a value visibly detached from the bulk of the data.
definitions.simple: a point living apart from the rest of the data.

definitions.text: an outlier is farther than 1.5 box-widths from Q1/Q3. Robust, does not depend on the mean.
definitions.simple: farther than one and a half "boxes" from the middle — an outlier candidate.

definitions.text: clip extreme values to a threshold (e.g. p1/p99) while keeping the observation in the data.
definitions.simple: do not throw it out — trim it to an allowed ceiling.

definitions.text: take the logarithm to compress the long tail and pull the data toward symmetry.
definitions.simple: squeezes huge values so the tail stops driving the analysis.

decision: A decision about an outlier is a decision about its nature: a data error — fix it; a real "big customer" — keep it and change the method, not the data.
practice: Before any analysis, an analyst draws a box plot or histogram and checks the outliers. Then separates: input errors get fixed, real extremes get handled deliberately (robust measures, capping, log), and the decision is documented so the analysis stays reproducible.
Sometimes the outlier IS the goal: a fraudulent transaction, a failing server, anomalous demand. Then you do not remove it — you hunt for it; a whole field of anomaly detection is built on this.
realLife: In money and response times outliers are a fact of life, not a rarity: there is always a pair of giant orders or hung requests. That is why medians and percentiles are the default there.
In machine learning uncleaned erroneous outliers drag the model toward themselves (especially with squared error), while real ones are, on the contrary, informative; confusing the two is expensive.
assumptions: The 1.5·IQR rule and the |z|>3 threshold are heuristics, not truth: different distributions have different "normal" shares of outliers. On heavily skewed data 1.5·IQR will flag many perfectly normal tail values.
Removing outliers changes conclusions and easily turns into result-fitting. Every removal must be justified by the nature of the data and documented.
